Git

Open Source

JavaScript

Software Development

Java

The Git workflow I naturally learned after a year of contributing to open source.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

When I made my first open-source contribution, I knew exactly one Git command with confidence:
git statusEverything else was either copied from Stack Overflow, searched on Google, or patiently explained by AI.
Today, after contributing to a large open-source project for over a year, Git has become second nature not because I memorized dozens of commands, but because every command solved a real problem.
If you’re planning to contribute to open source, you don’t need to learn every Git command. You need to understand the commands you’ll actually use throughout a contributor’s workflow.
This article covers exactly those commands and, more importantly, when and why you’ll use them.

1. Cloning the Repository
Your journey starts by creating a local copy of the project.
git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/<repository>.gitThis downloads the repository to your machine.
Before writing a single line of code, verify where your repository points.
git remote -vTypical output:
origin https://github.com/yourusername/project.git
origin https://github.com/yourusername/project.git (push)For open source, you’ll usually be working on a fork of the original project.
That means you’ll also want to add the original repository as upstream.
git remote add upstream https://github.com/original/project.gitVerify it:
git remote -vNow you’ll have:

• origin → your fork

• upstream → the official repository
Understanding this distinction is one of the first things every open-source contributor should learn.

2. Syncing With the Latest Changes
Open-source repositories move fast.
Before starting any new issue, always synchronize your local repository.
Fetch the latest changes:
git fetch upstreamSwitch to your main branch:
git switch master(or main, depending on the project)
Update it:
git merge upstream/masterSome projects prefer rebasing instead:
git rebase upstream/masterKeeping your branch updated early prevents painful merge conflicts later.

3. Creating a Feature Branch
Never work directly on master or main.
Create a dedicated branch for every issue.
git switch -c issue-123-fix-validationUsing meaningful branch names makes your workflow much easier to manage.

4. Checking What Changed
Probably the command I still use the most.
git statusIt tells you:

• modified files

• staged files

• untracked files

• current branch
Before every commit…
After every merge…
Before every push…
I still type:
git status
5. Reviewing Your Own Changes
Before staging anything, review your code.
git diffThis simple habit catches:

• accidental formatting

• debug statements

• unnecessary changes

• forgotten edits
Think of it as reviewing your own pull request before someone else does.

6. Staging Changes
Stage everything:
git add .Or stage only specific files:
git add src/main/java/MyClass.javaI generally prefer staging specific files in open source because it avoids committing unrelated changes.

7. Writing Good Commits
Commit your work.
git commit -m "Issue #123: Improve validation for empty input"A good commit message should explain what changed, not just that something changed.
Avoid messages like:
fix
changes
updated codeFuture contributors — including your future self — will thank you.

8. Updating an Existing Commit
Forgot a file?
Need to improve the commit message?
Instead of creating another unnecessary commit:
git commit --amendThis became one of my most frequently used commands during pull request reviews.

9. Saving Work Without Committing
Sometimes you’re halfway through implementing something when another task comes up.
Instead of making an incomplete commit:
git stashRetrieve it later:
git stash popThink of stash as a temporary shelf for unfinished work.

10. Viewing Commit History
To see a clean commit history:
git log --onelineThis is incredibly useful when:

• debugging

• understanding previous changes

• preparing a rebase

• reviewing project history

11. Rebasing Your Branch
One command that initially intimidated me was:
git rebaseIn open source, maintainers often ask contributors to rebase their branch before merging.
Why?
Because it keeps the project’s history clean.
A typical workflow looks like this:
git fetch upstreamgit rebase upstream/masterIf conflicts occur:
Resolve them.
Stage the resolved files.
Continue:
git rebase --continueAt first, rebasing felt complicated.
Today, it’s simply part of my normal workflow.

12. Resolving Merge Conflicts
Every contributor eventually sees this message:
This branch has conflicts that must be resolved.
Don’t panic.
Merge conflicts aren’t mistakes.
They’re simply Git asking you to choose between two competing changes.
The workflow is straightforward:
git fetch upstreamgit rebase upstream/masterResolve conflicts.
Then:
git add .git rebase --continueEvery merge conflict teaches you a little more about Git.

13. Pushing Your Changes
Push your branch.
git push origin issue-123-fix-validationIf you’ve rebased your branch, Git history has changed.
You’ll usually need:
git push --force-with-leaseNotice that I recommend --force-with-lease, not --force.
It’s much safer because it prevents accidentally overwriting someone else’s work.

14. Commands I Use Almost Every Day
| Purpose | Command |
| ---------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| Check repository state | `git status` |
| Review changes | `git diff` |
| Stage changes | `git add` |
| Commit changes | `git commit` |
| Amend the last commit | `git commit --amend` |
| View commit history | `git log --oneline` |
| Save unfinished work | `git stash` |
| Restore stashed work | `git stash pop` |
| Fetch latest changes | `git fetch upstream` |
| Rebase onto latest upstream branch | `git rebase upstream/master` |
| Push safely after rebasing | `git push --force-with-lease` |These commands cover almost everything I need in my day-to-day open-source workflow.

Git Is More Than Commands
One of the biggest lessons open source taught me is that Git isn’t about memorizing commands.
It’s about understanding the workflow.
Every command exists because it solves a real collaboration problem.

• git diff helps you review your own work before asking others to review it.

• git rebase keeps project history clean.

• git stash lets you pause work without losing progress.

• git log tells the story of a project's evolution.

• git commit --amend helps you present a cleaner contribution.
When you start contributing consistently, these commands stop feeling like syntax to remember.
They become tools you naturally reach for because you’ve encountered the situations they were designed to solve.
If you’re new to open source, don’t try to memorize Git.
Contribute regularly.
Review other people’s pull requests.
Resolve merge conflicts.
Fix mistakes.
Over time, Git stops being a list of commands and becomes an essential part of how you collaborate as a software engineer.
